free_text: 3He(t,t)3He elastic scattering measurements from
9 to 17 MeV
LASL polarized-triton source and
FN tandem Va de Graaff accel.
A gas target.
Counter telescopes containing two or three
silicon surface-barrier detectors.
Two telescopes used at 9, 13, 15 and 17 MeV consisting
of 150 mu-m dE (50 mu-m for the 9 MeV) and 1000 mu-m
E-detector.
At 11 and 16 MeV three detectors used. At 11 MeV
thicknesses were 17, 75 and 300 mu-m and at 16 MeV, the
detectors were 40, 150 and 1000 mu-m thick.
Compilation produced by Arzamas RFNC-VNIIEF
CNPD: corrected 4-dig. year and lower cases.
SD:volume of the ref. corrected: 16 -> 15.
SF9=EXP deleted from REACTION code in Subent 002.
Subents 003, 004 added.
The relative errors, arise mainly from the
following:
(1) uncertainties in reading the target gas pressure
from the Wallace-Tiernan gauge,
(2) statistical errors in the number of counts,
(3) errors in the setting of peak and background gates
(4) random pressure and temperature fluctuations
in the target,
(5) beam-current integration, and
(6) beam-position instability and/or detector-position
uncertainty
Scale error errors for the cross section may
be traced to
(1) calibration error in the pressure gauge,
(2) calibration error in the current integrator,
(3) purity of the target gas, and
(4) uncertainty in die geometry factor
Table 3
SD:SF9=EXP deleted from REACTION code.
Title DATA-ERR1 -> ERR-SYS. Data corrected according
to the Table 1.
The relative errors, arise from the
following:
(I) statistical errors in the peaks, including the
error due to background subtraction;
(2) random errors in setting the background gates
under the peaks of interest;
(3) random errors in the measurement of the beam
polarization by the quench-ratio method; and
(4) random systematic errors, such as position
instability in the beam. This value for the random
systematic error is based on experience with
repeatability of data for this type of experiment.
DATA-ERR are obtained by quadratically combining the
contributions of (1), (2), and (3) together with
0.005, which be a reasonable estimate of the errors
due to (4).
Scale error. The normalization or scale error
arises solely from errors in determining the absolute
value of the beam polarization using the quench-ratio
technique, since all other scale errors cancel for data
taken with symmetric geometry
Table 3
SD
Between 15 and 17 MeV, the angular distribution change
shape. To elucidate this change, excitation functions
from 14.42 to 17.22 MeV at lab angles of 22.5 and
37.5 degr. were measured. By measuring both tritons and
3He recoil particles at these angles, excitation
functions for four c.m. angles were obtained. These
data chow only gradual trends upward or downward.
The relative errors, arise from the
following:
(I) statistical errors in the peaks, including the
error due to background subtraction;
(2) random errors in setting the background gates
under the peaks of interest;
(3) random errors in the measurement of the beam
polarization by the quench-ratio method; and
(4) random systematic errors, such as position
instability in the beam. This value for the random
systematic error is based on experience with
repeatability of data for this type of experiment.
DATA-ERR are obtained by quadratically combining the
contributions of (1), (2), and (3) together with
0.005, which be a reasonable estimate of the errors
due to (4).
Scale error. The normalization or scale error
arises solely from errors in determining the absolute
value of the beam polarization using the quench-ratio
technique, since all other scale errors cancel for data
taken with symmetric geometry.
